title: Magnetic Particle Testing: Fundamentals
narrationScript: Magnetic particle testing turns the part itself into a magnet and lets the magnet point at its own flaws. Flux flows easily through ferromagnetic steel, but a discontinuity at or near the surface interrupts it and forces some flux out of the part as a leakage field. Iron particles pile up at that leak and draw a visible line around the flaw. This lesson builds the physics underneath that trick: flux, permeability, and the hysteresis loop that gives steel a magnetic memory — retentivity and coercive force. Then we put electric current to work. Current through the part wraps a circular field around it and reveals axial discontinuities. Current in a surrounding coil drives a longitudinal field and reveals transverse ones. Flux must cross the flaw, so full coverage takes two perpendicular shots. Alternating current hugs the surface; rectified current reaches deeper. Finally, demagnetization: why residual fields cause trouble downstream, when removal is required, and how a diminishing, reversing field walks the steel's magnetic memory back to zero.

sections.heading: Flux Leakage: How a Magnetized Part Finds Its Own Flaws
sections.body:
Magnetic particle testing (MT) magnetizes a **ferromagnetic** part and applies finely divided ferromagnetic particles — dry powder or a wet suspension — while a magnetic field is in the part. Sound metal is an easy highway for magnetic flux. A discontinuity at or near the surface is not: the air, slag, or oxide filling the flaw has a permeability hundreds of times lower than the surrounding steel, so flux must either crowd beneath the flaw or break out of the surface. The flux that breaks out forms a local **leakage field** with its own tiny north and south poles. Particles are pulled to that leak, bridge it, and hold — building a visible **indication** wider than the flaw itself, which is exactly what makes a tight crack visible to the eye.

MT is fast and inexpensive, and — unlike liquid penetrant — it does not require the flaw to be open to the surface; it can even find discontinuities under a thin nonconductive coating, though sensitivity falls as coating thickness grows. Three boundaries define the method:

- **Ferromagnetic materials only.** Iron, nickel, cobalt, and most carbon and low-alloy steels qualify. Aluminum, copper, titanium, and austenitic stainless steels do not — and no amperage setting changes that.
- **Surface and slightly subsurface flaws.** Sensitivity is highest for discontinuities open to the surface and falls rapidly with depth; with DC-type current, a flaw a few millimeters down can still show under favorable conditions. A sharp, dense, tightly held buildup says surface; a broad, weak, fuzzy buildup says subsurface.
- **Indications form where flux crosses the flaw, not where it runs alongside it** — the orientation rule developed below, and the reason every MT procedure controls field direction.

sections.caption: A magnetized bar with two flaws. The surface crack interrupts the flux sharply: strong leakage, dense particle mound. The subsurface void leaks a weaker, broader field, so its indication is wide and fuzzy — and a deeper flaw would not show at all.

sections.heading: The Field Itself: Flux, Permeability, and Material Classes
sections.body:
Picture any magnetic field as **lines of flux** — a drawing convention with rules that do real predictive work. Flux lines form continuous closed loops, never cross one another, follow the path of least magnetic resistance (**reluctance**), and pack densest where the field is strongest. Outside a magnet they run from the north pole to the south pole; inside, they complete the loop from south to north.

Two quantities and their ratio describe what current does to steel. **Magnetizing force (H)** is the field the current applies, expressed in amperes per meter or oersteds. **Flux density (B)** is the material's response — how much flux is actually carried per unit area, in teslas or gauss. Their ratio, **permeability (μ = B/H)**, measures how easily a material conducts flux; reluctance is its opposite number, the magnetic analog of electrical resistance.

Permeability sorts all materials into three classes:

- **Ferromagnetic** — iron, nickel, cobalt, and most carbon and low-alloy steels. Their internal magnetic **domains** align with an applied field, multiplying it; relative permeabilities run from hundreds to several thousand. These are the only MT-testable materials.
- **Paramagnetic** — aluminum, magnesium, austenitic stainless steel: very weakly attracted, relative permeability barely above 1.
- **Diamagnetic** — copper, gold, bismuth: very weakly repelled, relative permeability slightly below 1.

Ferromagnetism is also temperature-limited: above the **Curie point** — about 770 °C (1418 °F) for iron — thermal agitation randomizes the domains and the material behaves paramagnetically until it cools. That fact returns at the end of this lesson as the one demagnetization method that is total.

sections.heading: Hysteresis: Steel Has a Magnetic Memory
sections.body:
Plot flux density B against magnetizing force H for a piece of steel and you get the most useful curve in this method. Starting from a dead-unmagnetized state, the **virgin curve** climbs steeply — domains aligning — then flattens at **saturation**, where essentially all domains point with the field and more H buys almost no more B.

Now reduce H back to zero. B does not follow it down. The flux density remaining with the magnetizing force removed is the **residual magnetism**, and the material's capacity to hold it is its **retentivity (Br)**. To force B back to zero you must push H in the *reverse* direction; the reverse force required is the **coercive force (Hc)**. Keep going and the steel saturates in the opposite polarity — cycle the current and the trace closes into the **hysteresis loop**.

The loop's shape is the material's magnetic personality:

- **Magnetically soft** steels (annealed, low-carbon) draw a tall, narrow loop — easy to magnetize, little residual field, easy to demagnetize.
- **Magnetically hard** steels (high-carbon, heat-treated alloys) draw a wide loop — high retentivity and high coercive force. They hold a strong residual field and give it up grudgingly.

Three working consequences. The **continuous technique** — particles applied while the magnetizing current flows — gives maximum sensitivity, because B is always higher with H applied than after it is removed. The **residual technique**, examining after the current stops, is workable only on high-retentivity steels. And demagnetization, when required, is fundamentally a fight against coercive force — wide-loop steels fight hardest.

sections.title: Flux must cross the flaw
sections.body: Leakage — and therefore the indication — is strongest when flux crosses a discontinuity at **90°**. Detection stays dependable out to roughly **45°** from perpendicular; a flaw lying parallel to the flux barely disturbs it and is effectively invisible. Because flaw orientation is unknown in advance, complete examination requires magnetizing in **two directions at right angles** — in practice, a circular shot and a longitudinal shot of the same part.

sections.heading: Circular Magnetization: Current Through — or Inside — the Part
sections.body:
Electric current is the practical way to magnetize, and the **right-hand rule** is the compass: point the right thumb along the conventional current and the fingers curl in the direction of the flux circling it. Around any straight conductor, flux forms concentric circles — strength proportional to the current, falling off with distance outside the conductor. Inside a solid conductor carrying direct current, field strength is zero at the center and maximum at the surface; with alternating current, the **skin effect** crowds both current and flux toward the surface.

Pass the current through (or inside) the part and you get a **circular field** wrapping its circumference — the field that reveals **axial (lengthwise) discontinuities**:

- **Head shot.** The part is clamped between contact pads and current flows end to end. A common starting range is **300–800 A per inch (12–32 A/mm) of part diameter**, with the governing procedure setting the value. Contact surfaces must be clean and clamped firmly — poor contact means arcing and burns.
- **Central conductor.** Hollow or ring-shaped parts are threaded onto an insulated current-carrying bar. No current flows through the part itself — no arc risk on finished surfaces — and the field is strongest at the bore, so inside-diameter flaws (invisible to a head shot's external field) are examined directly, along with the OD. With the conductor laid **offset** against the bore wall, the effectively examined arc of circumference is about **four times the conductor diameter**; the part is rotated and shot again with overlap until the full circumference is covered.
- **Prods.** Hand-held contacts pass current through a local region of a large part or weld, creating a distorted circular field between the tips. Typical practice is roughly **90–125 A per inch of prod spacing**, with spacing about 75–200 mm (3–8 in.). The price is arc-burn risk at the tips, which is why prods are prohibited on many finished and aerospace components.

sections.caption: The two workhorse field directions. A head shot's circular flux crosses axial flaws and misses transverse ones; a coil's longitudinal flux does the reverse. Neither alone is a complete examination — full coverage takes both, 90° apart.

sections.heading: Longitudinal Magnetization — and Choosing the Current
sections.body:
Wrap the current *around* the part instead and the geometry flips. A **coil or cable wrap** obeys the same right-hand rule applied to its windings: flux runs **along the part's axis**, and the field now reveals **transverse discontinuities**. Coil strength is rated in **ampere-turns (NI)** — amperage times the number of turns — and its effectiveness depends on the part's length-to-diameter ratio. The working window is roughly **L/D between 2 and 15** (short parts are extended with pole pieces; above 15, use 15), and one widely used starting rule for a part laid against the inside wall of a low fill-factor coil is **NI ≈ 45,000 ÷ (L/D)**. The strongly magnetized zone extends only about **230 mm (9 in.) to either side of the coil's center**, so long parts are magnetized and examined in successive, overlapping shots.

A **yoke** reaches the same longitudinal result indirectly: current through a coil wound on a U-shaped core magnetizes the core, and the part completes the magnetic circuit between the two poles. Flux runs pole-to-pole through the part — revealing flaws perpendicular to the pole-to-pole line — with no current through the part and therefore no arc risk.

The **type of current** sets how deep the field lives:

- **AC** — the skin effect confines flux near the surface, so AC finds *surface* discontinuities only. Its reversals vibrate the particles, giving the best particle mobility, and its residual field is shallow and easy to remove.
- **Half-wave rectified DC (HWDC)** — pulsating, one-directional current that combines useful penetration with strong particle mobility; the favorite with dry powder on welds.
- **Full-wave rectified DC (FWDC)** — the steadiest waveform and the deepest penetration, best for slightly subsurface flaws. The trade: DC-type magnetization leaves the deepest residual field, which is exactly what makes demagnetization hard.

sections.heading: Demagnetization: Why, When, and How
sections.body:
Steel's magnetic memory outlives the test, and a residual field can cause real trouble downstream: machining chips cling to the work and mar finishes or damage tooling; **arc blow** deflects the arc in subsequent welding; residual fields disturb aircraft compasses and nearby instruments; rotating assemblies and bearings attract hard ferrous debris and wear; and a leftover field can interfere with plating, cleaning, or a later magnetization in a different direction. Demagnetization is **not** automatic, though. It is generally unnecessary when the material has low retentivity, when the part will be heated above the Curie point in subsequent processing, when it will immediately be re-magnetized for another examination, or when the residual field has no effect on the part's function — the governing specification or written procedure makes the call.

The principle behind every practical method is the same: apply a magnetizing force strong enough to overcome the coercive force — as a rule, at least as strong as the field that magnetized the part — then **reverse its direction repeatedly while shrinking it to zero**. Each reversal traces a smaller hysteresis loop, spiraling the residual flux density down toward nothing.

- **AC coil pull-through** — the everyday method. The part passes through an energized AC coil and is withdrawn slowly, about 1 m (3 ft) or more, before the current is switched off; the receding field supplies the diminishing reversals. The skin effect limits how deep AC can reach.
- **AC step-down** — the part stays in the coil while current is stepped to zero; same depth limitation.
- **Reversing step-down DC** — the field direction is flipped and reduced step by step. This reaches the full cross-section and is the method for heavy sections holding deep DC residual fields.
- **Heating above the Curie point** (~770 °C for iron) — the only *complete* demagnetization, practical only when heat treatment is planned anyway.

Verify the result with a field indicator or Hall-effect gauss meter; a common acceptance limit is **3 gauss (240 A/m) maximum** unless the governing specification says otherwise. Demagnetize long parts oriented **east–west**, so the Earth's field (~0.5 gauss) cannot re-magnetize them along their length as the applied field collapses. And remember the trap: a circularly magnetized part keeps its flux loop almost entirely *inside* the metal — a near-zero meter reading on such a part proves very little.

figures.title: Flux leakage at surface and subsurface discontinuities
Head shot on a shaft: which discontinuity shows?

interactive.title: Orient the Field to Find the Crack
interactive.intro: A head shot drives current end-to-end through this shaft, wrapping a circular field around its circumference. Click the discontinuity this shot will reveal most strongly.

regions.label: A — axial seam on the surface
regions.feedback: Correct. The seam runs along the axis, square across the circular flux — maximum leakage and a sharp, dense indication. Circular fields find axial discontinuities.

regions.label: B — transverse crack
regions.feedback: This crack lies parallel to the circular flux lines, so it barely disturbs them — little leakage, no dependable indication. It needs a longitudinal field from a coil or yoke.

regions.label: C — mid-wall inclusion
regions.feedback: Too deep. Its leakage spreads and dies before reaching the surface — MT is a surface and slightly-subsurface method. A volumetric method such as UT or RT owns this flaw.

regions.label: D — sound surface
regions.feedback: Flux flows undisturbed inside sound metal: nothing leaks, so particles do not hold. A clean background is exactly what lets real indications stand out.

checkQuestions.question: Magnetic particle testing can form an indication only where a discontinuity:
options: is open to the surface so that particles can settle into the cavity itself
interrupts the flux and forces a leakage field out at or near the surface
lies deep in the cross-section where flux density is greatest
is oriented parallel to the magnetic lines of flux
checkQuestions.explanation: Particles are held by leakage flux, not trapped in the cavity — which is why MT can show flaws under thin coatings and slightly below the surface. Deep flaws and flux-parallel flaws produce little or no surface leakage, so they go unseen.

checkQuestions.question: A solid steel shaft receives a head shot — current passed end to end between contact pads. The field produced, and the discontinuities it reveals best, are:
options: a longitudinal field, revealing transverse cracks
a circular field, revealing transverse cracks
a circular field around the circumference, revealing axial (lengthwise) discontinuities
a longitudinal field, revealing axial seams
checkQuestions.explanation: By the right-hand rule, flux circles the current path — current along the axis wraps a circular field around the shaft. Axial flaws cross that circumferential flux at about 90° and leak strongly; transverse flaws lie parallel to it and stay hidden.

checkQuestions.question: On the hysteresis loop, the flux density remaining in a part after the magnetizing force has returned to zero is the material's:
options: retentivity (residual magnetism)
coercive force
permeability
reluctance
checkQuestions.explanation: Residual magnetism — the measure of retentivity — is the flux density left at H = 0. Coercive force is the reverse magnetizing force needed to drive that flux density to zero; permeability is the ratio B/H; reluctance is opposition to flux.

checkQuestions.question: Which magnetizing current is the best choice when the concern is discontinuities slightly below the surface?
options: Alternating current, because the skin effect concentrates flux below the surface
Alternating current, because its reversals give particles maximum mobility
Any current type — penetration depends only on amperage, not waveform
Full-wave (or half-wave) rectified current, because its steady component magnetizes deeper into the cross-section
checkQuestions.explanation: The skin effect confines AC flux near the surface, so AC is a surface-only examination — its excellent particle mobility does not change that. Rectified DC-type currents penetrate deeper, which is why they are chosen for slightly subsurface flaws.

checkQuestions.question: A heavy shaft was magnetized with full-wave rectified DC. Which approach is most likely to remove its deep residual field?
options: Pass it slowly through an energized AC demagnetizing coil
Apply reversing, step-down DC demagnetization
Store it oriented east–west for several days
Wipe the surface with the poles of an AC yoke
checkQuestions.explanation: AC demagnetization — coil or yoke — is depth-limited by the skin effect, and east–west storage merely avoids picking up the Earth's field. A deep residual field from DC magnetization calls for a reversing DC field stepped down to zero, which works the full cross-section.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic Examination Topical Outline §2.5.1 (MT fundamentals: magnetic field principles, magnetization by means of electric current, demagnetization)
ASTM E1444/E1444M, Standard Practice for Magnetic Particle Testing (current-level guidance, coil ampere-turn rules, central conductor coverage, residual field limits)
ASTM E709, Standard Guide for Magnetic Particle Testing
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7, Magnetic Particle Examination (prod technique amperage and spacing)
